_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-10 Heat Emergencies (Revised 03/17/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-10: Heat Emergencies — MDHHS Approved 3/17/25, Reviewed 2025

sections.title: Heat Cramps
points: Follow General Pre-Hospital Care-Treatment Protocol; for pediatric patients, use Michigan MEDIC Cards when applicable
Determine the extent and duration of heat exposure and remove the patient from the hot environment
Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia per the Altered Mental Status protocol when indicated
Heat cramps: remove from the hot environment and give oral liquids or sports rehydration solution if the patient is alert and able to swallow

sections.title: Heat Exhaustion
points: Remove the patient from the hot environment and place them in a cool environment
Remove tight clothing; fan the patient, use air conditioning, and avoid chilling or inducing shivering
If the patient is not nauseated, not altered, and has no abdominal pain, provide cool oral fluids; avoid carbonated, alcoholic, or caffeinated drinks
If nausea or vomiting is present, refer to the Nausea and Vomiting protocol
If IV/IO access is indicated: Adults may receive NS or LR wide open up to 1 L; Pediatrics may receive NS or LR up to 20 mL/kg

sections.title: Heat Stroke
points: Heat stroke is a true emergency; aggressive cooling may take priority over transport
Remove the patient from the hot environment, remove tight clothing, and begin immediate cooling
Transport semi-reclined with the head elevated when tolerated
Refer to the Nausea and Vomiting protocol when needed
If IV/IO access is indicated: Adults may receive NS or LR wide open up to 1 L; Pediatrics may receive NS or LR up to 20 mL/kg
Contact Medical Control for heat stroke management guidance

sections.title: Exertional Heat Stroke
points: Cool quickly by ice or cold-water immersion when available
If immersion is not available, apply cold water over the entire skin surface with continuous fanning
Apply ice packs over as much of the body as possible; effective locations include palms, soles, and cheeks
COOL FIRST — TRANSPORT SECOND when possible; if scene plus transport time is expected to exceed 30 minutes, begin on-scene immersive cooling before evacuation
Continue cooling during transport — do not stop cooling en route
IV/IO fluid administration should not delay rapid whole-body cooling
If the patient is in water, ECG lead cables may be placed in the water for monitoring
Treat seizures per the Seizures protocol

key_drugs.name: Normal Saline IV/IO (Heat Emergencies)
key_drugs.dose: Adults: wide open up to 1 L; Pediatrics: up to 20 mL/kg
key_drugs.indication: Heat exhaustion or heat stroke when IV/IO fluid support is indicated
key_drugs.contraindications: Fluid overload; use caution in elderly or those with cardiac/renal history
key_drugs.notes: Cooling is the primary intervention in heat stroke. IV/IO fluids are supportive and should not delay rapid whole-body cooling.

deterioration_flags: Altered mental status with elevated temperature — heat stroke; aggressive cooling immediately; COOL FIRST, transport second when possible
Seizure activity — airway management, cooling, IV access (Specialist skill), contact Medical Control
Hypotension in heat stroke — IV fluid bolus (Specialist skill); continue aggressive cooling
If scene plus transport time for exertional heat stroke is expected to exceed 30 minutes — begin on-scene immersive cooling before evacuation
scope_notes: Cooling measures, cold-water immersion, cold water over skin with fanning, and environmental removal: all levels
Oral hydration for alert patients: all levels
IV/IO access and fluid administration: Specialist skill
ECG monitoring: per scope and available equipment; lead cables may be placed in water during immersion cooling
Seizure management: refer to appropriate protocol by level
